segments.purgatorio-26-g168: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a period) that ends the preceding line; there are no words to analyze grammatically.
segments.purgatorio-26-g169: Poi is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterwards' and opens the narrative sequence. s’ascose is a clitic reflexive construction: s’ is the contracted form of si (reflexive 'himself') attached before the verb, and ascose is a past simple (passato remoto) third-person singular of an archaic pronominal verb meaning 'to hide oneself' (so the whole means 'he hid himself'). Nel is the contraction of in + il ('in the'), introducing the location. Che begins a relative clause ('that/which') modifying foco. Li here is an old object pronoun 'him' (enclitic placement preserved from earlier syntax) and affina is present indicative third-person singular of affinare meaning 'refines' or 'purifies', so the relative clause says 'that refines him.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g108: Farotti is an archaic/clipped form whose sense here is best taken as a future construction meaning “it will do you” or “it will be to your advantage” (think of farà + ti). The phrase ben di me volere scemo groups as ben (adverb “well”) + di me (of me) + volere (a substantive/infinitive meaning “goodwill/to think”) with scemo as a predicate adjective applying to the understood object “me” — together: “it will be good for you to think me foolish.” Word order (verb-clitic + adverb + prepositional phrase + verbal noun + adjective) is archaic but parallels modern Italian word-order.
segments.purgatorio-26-g109: Son is the contracted form of sono (1st person singular present of essere) meaning “I am.” The names that follow are in apposition identifying the speaker: Guido Guinizzelli. The personal pronoun io is omitted because the verb son already marks the subject.
segments.purgatorio-26-g110: E già mi purgo is literal: e (“and”) + già (“already”) + mi purgo (1st person singular present of purgare with reflexive clitic mi) = “and already I purge/cleanse myself.” Per ben dolermi uses per + adverb ben + dolermi (infinitive with enclitic mi) and here means “by/through properly lamenting” (dolersi = lament). Prima ch’a lo stremo is an archaic sequence: prima che (contracted prima ch’ + a) + lo stremo = “before the final/extreme moment” (i.e. before my end). The whole clause states the means by which he is being purified: by sincere lamentation before death/end.
segments.purgatorio-26-g111: 'Quali' introduces a simile meaning 'as' or 'just as'; 'ne' is an older preposition meaning 'in' (here 'in the grief'); 'la tristizia di Ligurgo' is the noun phrase 'the sorrow of Lycurgus' (a mythic figure). The clause finishes with an impersonal/reflexive construction 'si fer due figli a riveder la madre' in which 'si' marks a reflexive/impersonal action and 'fer' is an archaic form indicating they made themselves or were moved to act (so: 'two sons were moved/took themselves to see their mother').
segments.purgatorio-26-g112: 'tal' = 'such/so' and the short clause 'tal mi fec' io' is a compact way of saying 'so did I' or 'I acted the same way'; 'fec' is the contracted poetic form of 'feci' (past of 'fare') and 'mi'/'io' make explicit the first-person subject and object emphasis ('I did this to myself' / 'I acted so').
segments.purgatorio-26-g113: 'ma non a tanto insurgo' — 'ma' = 'but'; 'non' negates; 'a tanto' = 'to such an extent'; 'insurgo' is first-person present 'I rise up/arise' (used here for vividness: 'I do not rise/go so far'). Grammatically, Dante uses the present 'insurgo' to temper the previous claim rather than a past tense.
segments.purgatorio-26-g114: This is a complex clause introduced by 'quand' (when). 'io odo nomar' = 'I hear (someone) named/called'; 'nomar' is an infinitive 'to name' used with 'odo' (I hear ... named). The objects follow: 'sé stesso il padre mio e de li altri miei miglior' = 'the father himself and my other betters', and the relative clause 'che mai rime d'amore usar dolci e leggiadre' modifies 'miglior' — Dante uses the relative 'che' plus an infinitive 'usar' to mean 'who ever used' (i.e., 'who at any time used sweet and graceful rhymes of love').
segments.purgatorio-26-g115: 'e sanza udire e dir pensoso andai lunga fïata rimirando lui' — 'e' = 'and'; 'sanza' = 'without'; the infinitives 'udire e dir' mean 'hearing or speaking' (i.e., without listening or saying anything). 'Pensoso' (thoughtful) qualifies the speaker; 'andai' is passato remoto 'I went/walked'; 'lunga fiata' = 'for a long time' (fiata = 'a time/space of time'); 'rimirando' is a present participle/gerund meaning 'gazing at' or 'contemplating' him. The sequence describes an action: I went on, thoughtful, gazing at him for a long time without hearing or speaking.
segments.purgatorio-26-g116: 'né' is the coordinating negation meaning 'nor' or 'not even', here linking the following clause negatively to what went before.
segments.purgatorio-26-g117: 'per lo foco' — 'per' here means 'because of' or 'on account of'; 'lo foco' = 'the fire' where 'foco' can be literal fire or figurative ardor/heat (shame, burning passion). In context it gives the reason for not approaching.
segments.purgatorio-26-g118: 'in là più m’appressai' — 'in là' = 'any farther' / 'further on'; 'più' = 'more' or here 'any more/any further'; 'm’ appressai' is passato remoto first person 'I approached/drew near' with the clitic 'm’' = 'mi' (to/for me). Due to the preceding 'né' the whole means 'nor did I come any closer.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g119: 'Poi che' = 'after/when'; 'di riguardar pasciuto fui' literally 'I was fed/satisfied from looking' — 'pasciuto' (from 'pascere') means 'sated' or 'having had enough'. The whole clause means 'after I had satisfied myself with looking' (pasciuto is predicative with 'fui').
segments.purgatorio-26-g120: 'tutto m.offersi pronto al suo servigio' — 'tutto' = 'completely'; 'm.’ offersi' is the reflexive passato remoto 'I offered myself' (mi offersi); 'pronto' = 'ready'; 'al suo servigio' = 'to his service'. The following 'con l’affermar che fa credere altrui' is an ablative/instrumental phrase: 'with the assertion (l’affermar) that makes others believe' where 'fa credere' is causative 'makes (people) believe' and 'altrui' = 'others'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g121: 'Ed elli a me:' — 'Ed' is a connective 'and'; 'elli' here is an emphatic/older form meaning 'he' (the speaker), and 'a me' = 'to me'. The phrase sets up direct speech ('and he to me:').
segments.purgatorio-26-g122: Inside the quotation: 'Tu lasci tal vestigio' — 'Tu' = 'you' (subject); 'lasci' = present 'you leave' (Dante often uses present for vivid comment); 'tal' = 'such'; 'vestigio' = 'a trace' or 'vestige'. Together: 'You leave such a trace.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g123: 'per quel ch'i' odo' — literal 'by that which I hear' = 'from what I hear' or 'as far as I can tell from what I hear'. 'per' = 'by/for/on account of', 'quel' = 'that', 'ch’ i' = 'that I', 'odo' = 'hear'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g124: 'in me' = 'in me' (literally 'inside me' — i.e., the trace remains in me/within me').
segments.purgatorio-26-g125: 'e tanto chiaro' — 'and so/so very clear' (tanto = 'so/so much', chiaro = 'clear/manifest'), qualifying the 'vestigio' just mentioned.
segments.purgatorio-26-g126: 'che Letè nol può tòrre né far bigio.' — 'che' introduces a result clause 'that'; 'Letè' = the river Lethe (metonym for oblivion); 'nol' = clitic contraction 'non + lo' (not it); 'può' = 'can'; 'tòrre' = infinitive 'to take away'; 'né' = 'nor'; 'far' = 'to make'; 'bigio' = 'gray/dull'. So: 'that Lethe cannot take it away nor make it dull.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g127: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but.' Se introduces a conditional clause 'if.' Le tue parole = 'your words' (definite article + possessive + noun). Or is the adverb 'now' (archaic spelling or contraction). Ver here is an older/poetic form related to 'vero' meaning 'true' or 'in truth' and qualifies the following verb; giuraro is a poetic/archaic verbal form from 'giurare' ('to swear') meaning 'sworn' or 'to have sworn'—taken together the phrase means 'if your words are now truly sworn.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g128: Dimmi is the imperative 'tell me' (di + mi fused into the verb). Che introduces an indirect question/content clause ('what' or 'that which'). È is the present indicative 'is.' Cagion means 'cause' or 'reason.' Per che = 'for which' / 'why' and introduces the subordinate clause that follows; dimostri is the 3rd-person singular present subjunctive (or present indicative in older usage) of 'dimostrare' used to express what is shown or evidenced. Nel dire e nel guardar is a locative idiom 'in (your) saying and in (your) looking/looks' (nel = in + article). D’avermi is the contracted form of 'di avermi' ('of/to have me'); caro is an adjective meaning 'dear' used predicatively: 'that you hold me dear.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g129: E io a lui is a simple clause 'And I to him' — E = 'and,' io = subject 'I,' a lui = indirect object 'to him.' This sets up direct speech introduced in the next segment.
segments.purgatorio-26-g130: Li dolci detti vostri = 'the sweet/delicate sayings of yours' (li = plural definite article, dolci = adjective 'sweet' agreeing in number/gender, detti = plural noun 'sayings/words,' vostri = plural possessive 'your'). In older Italian word order the article precedes the adjective and noun as here.
segments.purgatorio-26-g131: Che here is a relative conjunction 'which'/'that' introducing the following subordinate clause describing those 'sweet words.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g132: Quanto durerà l’uso moderno = a temporal clause 'as long as the modern/customary practice will last.' Quanto = 'how long' / 'as long as,' durerà is future indicative 'will last' (third singular), l’uso moderno = 'the modern usage/custom.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g133: Faranno cari ancora i loro incostri = 'will make dear/still render dear their incostri.' Faranno is future 'they will make' (third plural); cari is the predicate adjective 'dear' (plural, agreeing with incostri). Ancora = 'still' / 'again.' I loro incostri = 'their incostri' (incostri here means meetings/encounters or social gatherings); the phrase means that those gatherings will be cherished/valued as long as the present custom lasts.
segments.purgatorio-26-g134: '«O frate»,' is a direct address: 'O' = 'oh' (vocative marker) and 'frate' = 'brother' (religious or fraternal address).
segments.purgatorio-26-g135: 'disse,' is the simple past 'said' (passato remoto), introducing direct speech: 'he said.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g136: 'questi ch'io ti cerno col dito' — 'questi' = 'this one' (the person being indicated); 'ch’ io ti cerno' = 'whom I single out/point out to you' ('cernare' here = 'to select/discern/point out'); 'col dito' = 'with (the) finger' (i.e., by pointing). The clause is an appositive identifying who is meant.
segments.purgatorio-26-g137: 'e additò un spirto innanzi,' — 'e' = 'and'; 'additò' (past) = 'he pointed out' or 'he indicated'; 'un spirto' = 'a spirit/soul' (one of the spirits present); 'innanzi' = 'in front' or 'before us'. Grammatically it's the action that accompanies the previous 'col dito'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g138: 'fu miglior fabbro del parlar materno.' — 'fu' = past tense 'was'; 'miglior fabbro' = 'a better/finer craftsman' (figurative: skilled practitioner); 'del parlar materno' = 'of/ in motherly speech' (literally 'the motherly way of speaking' — i.e., skilled in maternal/affectionate speech). The sentence attributes to that spirit a particular skill in speaking in a motherly or affectionate manner.
segments.purgatorio-26-g139: The phrase 'Versi d’amore e prose di romanzi' is a fronted noun phrase giving the domain in which someone excelled; 'd’' is the elided form of 'di'. The verb 'soverchiò' is passato remoto, 3rd person singular, meaning 'surpassed/overwhelmed' and refers back to the person just named (so the verb is singular even though the fronted phrase is plural). 'tutti' is the direct object meaning 'all (others)'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g140: 'e lascia dir li stolti' is an idiomatic present-tense phrase meaning 'and let fools say' — 'lascia' (3rd sing. pres. of lasciare) functions like an impersonal 'allow/let' with the infinitive 'dir'. The relative clause that follows ('che quel di Lemosì credon ch’avanzi') reports what 'the fools' believe; 'credon' is 3rd pl. present 'they believe' and 'ch’avanzi' is a subordinate clause meaning 'that he/the one ... comes out ahead'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g1: Mentre che is a temporal conjunction (literally 'while that') marking an ongoing action; sì here means 'thus' or 'so' modifying the following phrase. Per + l'orlo means 'along/by the edge' (per used with a spatial sense). Word order is typical poetic placement: conjunction + manner + prepositional phrase.
segments.purgatorio-26-g2: Uno innanzi altro is an idiom meaning 'one before another' → 'one after another.' Innanzi functions as a prepositional/adverbial 'before' in this fixed phrase; the order emphasises procession.
segments.purgatorio-26-g3: Ce n'andavamo is a cliticized imperfect of andare: ce (there/at it) + ne (of it/from it) + andavamo 'we were going' — literally 'we were going along it/there.' The enclitics express location/partitive relation to the place just mentioned.
segments.purgatorio-26-g4: E spesso il buon maestro diceami: is an instance of Dante's compressed spelling: diceami = dicea + mi ('he used to say to me'). Diceami is third‑person imperfect with the clitic 'mi'. The phrase sets up reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-26-g5: Guarda: is an imperative interjection 'Look!' used to call attention; colon introduces the following admonition. Short, direct command typical in reported instructions.
segments.purgatorio-26-g6: Giovi ch’io ti scaltro is an impersonal construction: giovi = 'it is beneficial/ fitting'; ch’ = che 'that'; io ti scaltro is subordinate clause 'I warn/ make cautious you' (scaltro here is a rare/poetic verb form meaning to make crafty/alert). The sequence expresses purpose/advice.
segments.purgatorio-26-g7: Feriami il sole in su l’omero destro is a compact past narrative clause: feriami here functions as a past action affecting the speaker 'the sun struck me' (ferire = strike/hit); il sole is the subject; in su + l'omero destro = 'upon the right shoulder.' Note the clitic placement: feriami = ferìa + mi in Dantean orthography.
segments.purgatorio-26-g8: Che già introduces a relative clause and temporal/causal nuance: 'which already...' (già = already). The relative pronoun che links back to 'omero' (the shoulder) as the thing that was radiating.
segments.purgatorio-26-g9: Raggiando is a present participle (or gerundive notion) from raggiare 'to radiate/beam.' It modifies the shoulder (che già raggiando = 'which, already sending out rays').
segments.purgatorio-26-g10: Tutto l’occidente mutava in bianco aspetto di cilestro is a descriptive clause: mutava is imperfect 'was changing/turned'; tutto l'occidente 'the whole west (sky at sunset)'; in + noun expresses transformation into 'a white aspect of cilestro' (cilestro = sky‑blue). Word order (tutto subject then verb) is straightforward narrative description.
segments.purgatorio-26-g11: E io facea con l’ombra più rovente parer la fiamma: facea is imperfect of fare 'I would make/caused'; con l'ombra più rovente = 'with the hottest/most burning shadow'; parer = 'to appear' (infinitive), so the construction means 'I made the flame appear' or 'I made the flame seem (so) by the hot shadow.' The sequence uses causative fare + infinitive.
segments.purgatorio-26-g12: E pur a tanto indizio vidi molt’ ombre: e pur = 'and yet/even so'; a tanto indizio = 'at such a sign/indication'; vidi = perfect 'I saw'; molt' ombre = 'many shades' (molt' is an aphetic form of molte). The clause reports Dante's perception triggered by the sign.
segments.purgatorio-26-g13: Andando is a present participle/gerund 'going' that qualifies the main action ('as we went' / 'while we were walking'). It's used adverbially to link motion to the seeing.
segments.purgatorio-26-g14: Poner mente is an idiomatic phrase 'to pay attention' or 'to take heed' (literally 'to put mind'). Infinitive + noun: poner (to put) + mente (mind) yields 'to heed.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g15: Questa fu la cagion che diede inizio loro a parlar di me is a simple past statement: Questa fu = 'this was'; la cagion che diede inizio = 'the cause that gave beginning' → 'the reason that caused' ; loro a parlar di me = 'them to begin to speak of me' (loro is indirect object of the action 'gave beginning to'). Word order reflects Italian idiom 'dare inizio a qualcuno a fare qualcosa.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g16: E cominciarsi a dir: uses the reflexive/composite cominciarsi (to begin) plus a + infinitive; here it means 'and they began to say.' The reflexive marks the action as starting among the group.
segments.purgatorio-26-g17: «Colui non par corpo fittizio»: par is the present tense of parere 'to seem' (omitted from the listed words), so the clause means 'That one does not seem a feigned/fictitious body.' Negation non precedes the verb; colui is the demonstrative 'that one.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g18: Poi verso me = 'then/toward me' — poi gives sequence ('then') and verso me indicates the direction of the subsequent action (they turn toward Dante).
segments.purgatorio-26-g19: Quanto potëan farsi is an idiomatic clause: quanto = 'as much as' (extent), potëan (potéano) = imperfect 'they could,' and farsi (literally 'to make oneself' or here a passive/impersonal sense) yields 'as much as they could (make/bring about).' It qualifies the following action: 'as much as they could do.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g20: Certi si fero: certi = 'some' (some of them), si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic, and fero is an adjective 'fierce' (here used predicatively) — 'some made themselves fierce' or 'some assumed a fierce look.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g21: Sempre con riguardo di non uscir dove non fosser arsi is a caution phrase: sempre con riguardo = 'always with care'; di non uscir = 'not to go out/step'; dove non fosser arsi = 'where they would not have been burned' (fosser = archaic subjunctive/pluperfect form of essere + participle arsi 'burned'). It expresses their carefulness about stepping only where they'd be burned.
segments.purgatorio-26-g22: «O tu che vai, is a direct address: O = 'O/Oh' (vocative), tu 'you', che vai 'who go' (relative clause used in address). The inversion is typical for vocative openings.
segments.purgatorio-26-g23: Non per esser più tardo: non per + infinitive = 'not for the purpose of being'; esser più tardo = 'to be slower/later.' The clause denies a motive (i.e., 'not because you are slower').
segments.purgatorio-26-g24: Ma forse reverente: ma 'but/rather'; forse 'perhaps'; reverente 'reverent/deferential' — giving a possible reason for the listener's action (i.e., 'but perhaps out of reverence').
segments.purgatorio-26-g25: A li altri dopo = 'after the others' or 'following the others': a + definite article li (archaic form of i/they) + altri (others) + dopo (after). The phrase explains temporal/positional relation to the rest of the group.
segments.purgatorio-26-g26: Rispondi a me che ’n sete e ’n foco ardo is an imperative: rispondi 'answer (to) me'; che introduces the reason clause 'because' or 'for' (here 'for I burn'); ’n is the contracted form of in (in + vowel elision) meaning 'in thirst and in fire' — 'I burn with thirst and with flame' (ardo = 'I burn').
segments.purgatorio-26-g27: Né solo a me la tua risposta è uopo: 'Né solo a me' = 'not only for me'; la tua risposta = 'your answer'; è uopo = 'is necessary/is needed' (uopo = necessity, a somewhat archaic noun). The clause denies exclusivity of the request.
segments.purgatorio-26-g28: Ché tutti questi n’hanno maggior sete che d’acqua fredda Indo o Etïopo: ché = 'for/because'; tutti questi = 'all these (people)'; n' hanno = ne hanno = 'have of it' / 'have more of' (ne is partitive/object clitic); maggior sete = 'greater thirst'; che d'acqua fredda Indo o Etïopo = 'than (for) the cold water of the Indus or Ethiopia' — comparative structure using che 'than.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g141: A voce is a prepositional phrase (a + noun) meaning “to/at the voice” or here better “to the public voice/fame”; più ch’al ver is a comparative phrase “more than to the truth” (ch’ = che). Drizzan is an archaic present-tense form (drizzan = drizzano) meaning “they direct/turn” and li volti (old plural article li + volti, “faces”) is the direct object: literally “they turn their faces more toward the voice than toward the truth.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g142: E così (“and thus”) links the second clause. Ferman is an archaic 3rd-person plural present of fermare (ferman = fermano) meaning “they fix/hold”; sua oppinïone is the object (sua = their/its possessive before the noun). Prima ch’ introduces a subordinate temporal clause with a subjunctive sense: arte o ragion per lor s’ascolti = “before art or reason is/heard by them.” Here s’ is the reflexive/passive marker (s’ascolti = subjunctive passive or reflexive construction meaning ‘be listened to by them’), and per lor = “by them.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g143: Così fer is a concise, archaic past narrative: fer stands for a past action (like fecer/fecero) meaning “thus did.” Molti antichi = “many ancients” (i.e., many older poets or authorities) and di Guittone indicates the target or subject of that treatment (about or toward Guittone). The phrase is a short narrative main clause: “Thus many ancients did (this) to Guittone.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g144: Di grido in grido is an idiomatic repeated prepositional phrase meaning “from cry to cry” or “from one report/fame to another.” Pur is an adverb (“still/yet”) and lui is the object pronoun “him.” Dando is a gerund (showing concomitant manner) “giving,” and pregio is its object (“praise/esteem”): together describing how they acted—“giving him praise from one outcry to another.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g145: Fin che introduces a limit (“until”) followed by a clause with the perfect tense ha vinto (auxiliary avere + past participle vinto) meaning “has overcome/defeated.” The clitic l’ (lo) is the direct object ‘him/it’ (referring back to Guittone or his reputation). Il ver “the truth” is the subject that ‘wins,’ and con più persone (“with more people”) indicates the means/supporter: “until the truth, with more people, prevailed over him.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g146: 'Or' = 'now'; 'se' introduces a conditional 'if'; 'tu hai' is 2nd person singular present indicative 'you have'; 'sì ampio privilegio' is the NP 'so/so great an/ample privilege' with 'sì' intensifying 'ampio'. Word order is normal (temporal + conditional + subject + verb + complement).
segments.purgatorio-26-g147: This is the subordinate clause of result/condition. 'che' = 'that/so that'; 'licito ti sia' uses the predicative adjective 'licito' with the verb 'sia' (present subjunctive of 'essere') and the clitic dative 'ti' = 'to you' — literally 'that it be lawful to you'. 'l’andare' is the infinitive used substantively ('the going') and functions as the action permitted. 'nel quale' introduces a relative clause ('in which') and the clause finishes with 'è Cristo abate del collegio' — 'Christ is abbot of the college'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g148: 'falli' is an imperative form meaning 'do (it) for him/her/it' built from 'fare' with an enclitic pronoun (here functioning as 'do for me' together with the following 'per me'); idiomatically here it means 'do for me' or 'say for me'. 'per me' marks the beneficiary. 'un dir d’un paternostro' is a noun phrase: 'a saying/recitation of one Paternoster (Our Father)' — i.e. 'say one Our Father for me'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g149: 'quanto' = 'as much as' or 'how much'; 'bisogna' is impersonal present (from 'bisognare') meaning 'is necessary'; the dative 'a noi' = 'for us'; 'di questo mondo' qualifies the necessity as applying to 'this world' (i.e. in this life). The whole phrase = 'as much as is necessary for us in this world.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g150: 'dove' = 'where' (introducing a clause of place/condition); 'poter peccar' is a fused infinitival phrase 'to be able to sin' (note contraction 'peccar' for 'peccare'); 'non è più nostro' literally 'is no longer ours' — an idiom meaning 'it is no longer within our power' or 'we no longer have the ability' (here: to sin).
segments.purgatorio-26-g151: 'Poi' is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'after that', used to sequence actions; it stands alone and connects this event to the previous narrative.
segments.purgatorio-26-g152: This clause gives a possible purpose: 'forse' = 'perhaps'; 'per' + infinitive ('dar') expresses purpose ('in order to give'); 'dar luogo altrui' is a fixed expression meaning 'to give place/way to someone else' (i.e. to let another go forward); 'secondo' here is an ordinal 'second' (the next person), and the relative 'che' introduces 'presso avea' = 'whom he had near' (imperfect 'avea' = 'he had' / 'was keeping near').
segments.purgatorio-26-g153: 'Disparve' is the literary/passato remoto form (3rd person singular) meaning 'he disappeared' or 'vanished'; 'per' here means 'through' (indicating movement by means of) and 'lo foco' = 'the fire' (article + noun).
segments.purgatorio-26-g154: 'Come' = 'as' / 'like' introduces a simile; 'per l'acqua' = 'through the water' ('per' = 'through'); 'il pesce' is the subject of the simile and 'andando' is a gerund of 'andare' describing simultaneous motion ('going'/'sinking'); 'al fondo' = 'to the bottom' ('al' = a + il, 'fondo' = bottom). Word order mirrors English simile structure.
segments.purgatorio-26-g29: Dinne is an imperative form (di + enclitic ne) meaning “say/tell of it” or “tell me of it.” com’ è che is the frozen interrogative phrase “how is it that,” with è (present indicative of essere) plus the conjunction che. fai is 2nd-person-singular present of fare (“you do/make”); here it introduces what the addressee causes or does to themselves. di te is a prepositional phrase “of/with yourself” (emphatic reflexive), parete is an archaic/poetic verb form (from paretere/parere) meaning roughly “turn/appear/position oneself” (used reflexively with di te), and al sol is the preposition a + article “toward the sun.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g30: pur come = “just/even as” (pur = even/just). tu non fossi ancora is a concessive clause with the imperfect subjunctive fossi (here used in a contrary-to-fact or hypothetical sense) meaning “as if you were not yet” or “as though you had not yet.” di morte intrato dentro da la rete is a past-participial phrase: intrato (archaic participle of entrare) + dentro (inside) + da la rete (by/into the net of death), i.e. “entered into death’s net.” The whole clause expresses appearance contrary to reality (“as if you had not yet been caught by death”).
segments.purgatorio-26-g31: Sì = “thus/so”; mi is the indirect object “to me”; parlava is the imperfect tense 3rd-person-singular of parlare, indicating ongoing or repeated past action (“spoke/was speaking”); un d’essi = “one of them” (d’ = di + essi). The imperfect sets the scene of someone addressing Dante.
segments.purgatorio-26-g32: e io mi fora già manifesto: e = “and”; io mi = emphatic subject + reflexive; fora is an archaic/descriptive conditional or irrealis form (Dante’s older morphology) expressing what would have been the case (“I would have been” / “I would have shown myself”); già = “already”; manifesto = predicate adjective “made plain/revealed.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g33: s’io non fossi atteso ad altra novità ch’apparve allora = a counterfactual conditional. s’io = se io (“if I”); non fossi atteso uses the imperfect subjunctive fossi + past participle atteso to express being detained/held back (“had not been held back”); ad altra novità = “by/for another novelty/sight”; ch’apparve allora = relative clause with apparve (passato remoto) “that appeared then.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g34: ché = “for/because”; per lo mezzo del cammino acceso = “through the middle of the path that was lit/ablaze” (mezzo = middle, acceso = lit/kindled); venne gente = venne (passato remoto of venire) + gente (collective noun) → “people came”; col viso incontro a questa = with their faces meeting/turned toward this one (col = con + il, incontro a = toward/meeting, questa = that woman/figure).
segments.purgatorio-26-g35: la qual = relative pronoun “which” referring back to the preceding person; mi fece a rimirar uses causative fare in the past (fece) + the construction a + infinitive (a rimirar) to mean “caused me to look again” or “made me turn to look”; sospeso is a predicate adjective meaning “suspended/held in suspense,” i.e. it made him pause in his gaze.
segments.purgatorio-26-g36: Lì veggio = “there I see” (veggio = present of vedere, used narratively). d’ogne parte = “from every side” (d’ = di); farsi presta = reflexive farsi + adjective presta (“make oneself ready”); ciascun’ ombra = “each shade” (ciascun’ = ciascuno with elision); e basciarsi una con una = reciprocal verb basciarsi (“kiss one another”), literally “one with one”; sanza restar = archaic sanza = senza (“without remaining/staying”).
segments.purgatorio-26-g37: contente a brieve festa = contente (pleased/content) + a (prepositional use = “with/at”) + brieve festa (“a short/brief feast”); the phrase describes the shades’ brief, contented rejoicing.
segments.purgatorio-26-g38: così = “thus”; per entro loro schiera bruna = per entro (archaic double preposition meaning “throughout/within”) + loro schiera bruna = “their dark/brown array/host.” s’ammusa l’una con l’altra formica is reflexive: s’ammusa (ammusarsi) describes a reciprocal or mutual action among the ants—here “one ant with another [does something together/mingles]” (the verb is archaic/poetic).
segments.purgatorio-26-g39: forse a spïar lor via e lor fortuna = forse (“perhaps/perchance”); a spïar (spïar with dieresis marks separate vowels) = infinitive “to spy upon/observe”; lor via e lor fortuna = “their way and their fortune” (lor = their). The phrase suggests the ants inspect the path and fate of the shades.
segments.purgatorio-26-g40: Tosto che = “as soon as”; parton is 3rd-person-plural present of partire (they depart/leave); l’accoglienza amica = the friendly reception/welcome (accoglienza is a collective noun; amica is an attributive adjective). The clause means “as soon as the friendly welcome leaves/ends.”
segments.purgatorio-26-g41: prima che ’l primo passo lì trascorra = prima che (“before” + subjunctive); ’l primo passo = “the first step”; lì = “there”; trascorra is the present subjunctive of trascorrere used after prima che to mean “before the first step has passed/is taken.” The subjunctive marks the not-yet-realized action.
segments.purgatorio-26-g42: sopragridar ciascuna s’affatica = sopragridar (an infinitive meaning roughly “to shout above/over” or “to shout out”) + ciascuna (each one); s’affatica = si + affatica (present, reflexive) “wears herself out/strains herself.” Together: each one exerts herself to call out loudly.
segments.purgatorio-26-g43: la nova gente: = “the new people” (la + nova + gente). Gente is singular in form but collective in meaning; this labels the speakers who will shout the following name.
segments.purgatorio-26-g44: «Soddoma e Gomorra»; = the shouted names “Sodom and Gomorrah.” The line records the outcry made by that group.
segments.purgatorio-26-g45: e l’altra: = “and the other (group):” (e = and; l’altra = the other [feminine/collective]). It introduces a different outcry from another group.
segments.purgatorio-26-g46: «Ne la vacca entra Pasife, = Ne (archaic/in literary use for in) la vacca = “into the cow”; entra = 3rd-singular present of entrare (“enters”); Pasife is the proper name Pasiphae (here placed after the verb for poetic word order). The clause describes the mythic entrance of Pasiphae into the cow.
segments.purgatorio-26-g47: perché ’l torello a sua lussuria corra = perché can mean “so that/because” here introducing purpose/explanation; ’l torello = the young bull; a sua lussuria = “to his lust” (a = toward); corra is present subjunctive of correre, used after perché in the purposive/explanatory sense (“that the bull may run” / “so that he may rush to his lust”).
segments.purgatorio-26-g157: El is the subject pronoun 'he' (Occitan/archaic form). cominciò is Italian passato remoto (3rd sing.) meaning 'began'. liberamente is an adverb modifying cominciò. a + infinitive (a dire) is the normal complement structure after cominciare: 'to begin to say.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g158: Tan is an intensifier 'so/so much'. m' is the clitic object for 'me' placed before the verb (common in Romance clitic syntax). abellis is an archaic/Occitan present form meaning 'they embellish/please' (3rd plural), with the person expressed on the verb; vostre cortes deman is the subject phrase 'your courteous request/plea' (vostre = your, cortes = courteous).
segments.purgatorio-26-g159: qu' introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. ieu is the subject pronoun 'I' (Occitan). The negation is no before the verb (no ... ni ...): no puesc 'I cannot', ni voill 'nor will I' (puesc and voill are archaic/Occitan first‑person present forms: 'I can' and 'I want/will'). me is the reflexive/object clitic ('myself') placed before the finite verbs. a + cobrire is an infinitival complement with a marking the indirect object 'from you' (a vos = to/at you), and cobrire is the infinitive 'to cover/conceal.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g160: Ieu is the subject pronoun 'I' (Occitan). sui is the first‑person singular present of 'to be' (I am) in the Occitan/archaic form; Arnaut is the proper name as the predicate noun: 'I am Arnaut.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g161: que is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause describing Arnaut. plor is a verb form 'I weep' (or a nominal/gerund sense 'weeping' in older usage) and e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. vau is the first‑person singular present of 'to go' (I go), and cantan here functions adverbially/participially 'singing' (go singing). The clause therefore means 'who weeps and goes (about) singing.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g162: consiros is a noun/verb form meaning 'deliberations/counsels' or 'what I consider'; vei is 'I see' (first person present). The phrase la passada folor uses passada as an adjectival/participial 'past/passed' modifying folor 'folly/frenzy', so 'the past folly' — the object seen by the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-26-g163: e 'and' links clauses; vei again is 'I see'. jausen is a form meaning 'lie/are placed' or 'rest' (here describing the state of the joy), lo joi is 'the joy', and qu' introduces the relative clause 'that ...' (qu’esper = that I hope/expect). The clause says 'and I see the joy that I expect as lying/placed [before me].'
segments.purgatorio-26-g164: denan is an adverb meaning 'before/previously/in advance' (here 'beforehand' or 'already'), specifying the temporal relation of the hoped‑for joy.
segments.purgatorio-26-g165: Ara is the adverb 'now'. vos prec is a fixed phrase 'I beg you' (prec is the verb/noun of pleading; here it functions as a first‑person present 'I beg/request' with the ethical dative 'vos' = 'you'). The clause is a direct appeal: 'Now I beg you.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g166: per introduces the ground/authority 'by/through'. aquella is the demonstrative 'that'. valor here means 'valor/bravery/value' and functions as the noun invoked ('by that valor'). que vos guida is a relative clause 'which guides you' (guida functions as a verb 'guides' here); al som de l’escalina is a prepositional phrase 'to the summit/top (som) of the little staircase (escalina)' with al = a + il 'to the'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g167: sovenha is an imperative or hortative form 'remember' (second person imperative or present subjunctive used as request). a temps 'in time' (a = to/at; temps = time). de ma dolor = 'of my sorrow' or 'my sorrow' (de + possessive phrase); ma is the possessive 'my' and dolor is 'sorrow/pain'. The whole is a plea: 'Remember (me/my sorrow) in time.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g155: 'Io' is the emphatic subject 'I'; 'mi fei' is a reflexive construction with the past tense of 'fare' (Dante's 'fei' = 'feci'), literally 'I made myself' and here meaning 'I moved/stepped'; 'al mostrato' = 'to the one shown/pointed out' (article + past participle used substantively); 'innanzi un poco' is an adverbial phrase 'a little forward' ('innanzi' = in front/forward, modified by 'un poco').
segments.purgatorio-26-g156: 'E dissi' = 'and I said' (passato remoto 'dissi'); 'ch’' = 'che' introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'; 'al suo nome' literally 'at his name' = 'at the mention/sound of his name'; in that clause the subject is 'il mio disire' ('my desire') and the verb 'apparecchiava' is imperfect ('was preparing'/'prepared'), with 'grazïoso loco' ('gracious/charming place') as the object. The imperfect here gives an ongoing or habitual past action in narration.
segments.purgatorio-26-g48: Poi is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterwards', used to mark the next action in the narrative; it stands alone as a sentence marker before the simile.
segments.purgatorio-26-g49: come introduces a simile 'like'; grue is the plural noun 'cranes' (birds); ch’ is the contracted relative pronoun 'che' = 'who/which' introducing the clause; a is the preposition 'to/toward'; le montagne Rife is a proper-name phrase ('the Rife mountains'); volasser is the imperfect/subjunctive-ish poetic form meaning 'flew' or 'were flying' (Dante uses -sser for subjunctive/poetic past sense); parte here means 'in part'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g50: e introduces the second half of the movement; parte repeats 'in part'; inver’ is an archaic contraction of 'inver'/'in verso', here 'toward'; l’ arene = 'the sands' (arene = sands/arena).
segments.purgatorio-26-g51: queste (feminine plural demonstrative) picks out one group ('these'); del = di + il 'of the/from the'; gel = 'frost' or 'cold' — here meaning 'affected by the cold'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g52: quelle (feminine plural demonstrative) picks out the other group ('those'); del 'of the'; sole = 'sun'; schife is an adjective meaning 'displeased/affected by' or 'scorched/disliked by' — here describing those driven by the sun. Poetically it marks contrast with 'del gel'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g53: l’una gente sen va: l’una (the one) + gente (group/people) are the subject; sen va is the reflexive/separable form of 'andarsene' meaning 'go away' or 'depart' (sen = se + ne, here indicating movement away).
segments.purgatorio-26-g54: l’altra (the other) is the subject; sen vene is Dantean compression of 'venire' with particle (here meaning 'come toward/arrive') — a matching motion contrasted with 'sen va'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g55: e = 'and'; tornan is the present plural 'tornano' (poetic form) meaning 'they return' — a simple indicative describing repeated motion.
segments.purgatorio-26-g56: lagrimando is a present participle/adverbial gerund 'weeping' (literally 'shedding tears'), modifying the returning action.
segments.purgatorio-26-g57: a’ primi canti = at/to the 'first songs' (primi = earliest/first; canti here = songs/chants); e al gridar = 'and to the shouting/cry'; che più lor si convene = relative clause 'that (which) most suits them' (convene = 'be fitting' or 'be appropriate to'). Together this says they return to the original chants and to the cry that best fits them.
segments.purgatorio-26-g58: e = 'and'; raccostansi is the reflexive present plural 'they draw themselves near' (raccostarsi = to come closer); a me = 'to me' (they approach the speaker).
segments.purgatorio-26-g59: come = 'as/like'; davanti = 'before' or 'as before' — here 'as they had done previously' (a temporal/recalling 'as earlier').
segments.purgatorio-26-g60: essi medesmi = 'the very same ones' (essi = they; medesmi = same/very same); che m’avean pregato = relative clause 'who had begged/implored me' (m’ = mi, indirect object; avean = avevano, imperfect 'had'; pregato = past participle of pregare, 'asked/implored').
segments.purgatorio-26-g61: attenti = 'attent' or 'intent' (adjective modifying them); ad ascoltar = 'to listen' (infinitive used after 'attenti'); ne’ lor sembianti = 'in their countenances/facial expressions' (ne’ = in the; lor = their; sembianti = looks/appearances).
segments.purgatorio-26-g62: Io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I' used to mark the speaker before his statement.
segments.purgatorio-26-g63: che introduces a relative clause 'who'; due volte = 'twice' (two times); avea visto = imperfect 'had seen' (avea = aveva, past imperfect auxiliary; visto = past participle); lor = 'their' (object/possessive); grato here means 'gracious/pleasant' or 'favour' — the phrase means 'who had twice seen their favor/grace'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g64: incominciai is the first-person singular past 'I began' (passato remoto in Dante's register), introducing the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-26-g65: «O = vocative 'O'; anime = 'souls' (addressing them); sicure d’aver = 'secure/certain of having' — 'sicure' means confident/certain, and d'aver is the infinitive 'of having' (here 'of possessing a state of peace').
segments.purgatorio-26-g66: quando che sia is a set phrase 'whenever it may be' or 'whenever it is', with sia in the subjunctive 'be' expressing uncertainty about time.
segments.purgatorio-26-g67: di pace stato = 'of a state of peace' (di = of; pace = peace; stato = state or condition), describing what the souls are secure of having.
segments.purgatorio-26-g68: non son rimase acerbe né mature le membra mie di là = literal word order: 'my limbs there were not left unripe nor ripe' (non ... son rimase = negative + compound verb meaning 'were not left/remaining'; acerbe = 'unripe/harsh'; né mature = 'nor mature'; le membra mie = 'my limbs'; di là = 'over there' or 'on that side'). Dante's order reflects poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-26-g69: ma = 'but'; son qui meco = 'they are here with me' (son = are; qui = here; meco = with me); col sangue suo e con le sue giunture = 'with their blood and with their joints' (col = con + il; sangue suo = literally 'its/his/their blood' but here 'their blood'; giunture = joints or joins), stating that the speaker's limbs exist with their bodily components in the present location.
segments.purgatorio-26-g70: Quinci sù vo = 'from here upward I go' (quinci = from here; sù = up; vo = 'vado' in Dantean form 'I go'); per non esser più cieco = 'in order not to be any more blind' (per = 'in order to', non esser = not to be, più cieco = any longer blind). The infinitive esser follows the per to express purpose.
segments.purgatorio-26-g71: donna è di sopra = 'there is a lady above' (donna = lady/woman; è = is; di sopra = on high/above); che m’acquista grazia = relative clause 'who gains/obtains grace for me' (m’ = mi 'for me'; acquista = 'obtains/wins'; grazia = grace/favor). The verb acquista has the sense 'obtain' or 'win favor' here.
segments.purgatorio-26-g72: per che ’l mortal per vostro mondo reco = 'through whom I carry the mortal through your world' (per che = 'by/through whom'; 'l mortal = the mortal/human being; per vostro mondo = through your world/through your lands/through your earthly life; reco = I bear/carry — 'reco' is the 1st person present/poetic form of 'recare' = 'to carry/bring').
segments.purgatorio-26-g73: Ma = 'but'; se = 'if'; la vostra maggior voglia = 'your greater/stronger desire' (maggior = comparative 'greater'); sazia = 'is satisfied' (sazia = from saziare, here adjective/verb sense 'satiated/fulfilled'); tosto divegna = 'soon becomes' or 'quickly comes to pass' (tosto = soon/quickly; divegna = poetic/historical form of 'diviene' = becomes).
segments.purgatorio-26-g74: sì che = 'so that'; ’l ciel v’alberghi = 'the heaven may harbour you' (l' ciel = the sky/heaven; v’ = vi = you; alberghi = subjunctive/hortative sense 'may lodge/receive'); ch’è pien d’amore = 'which is full of love' (ch’ = che = which; è = is; pien d’amore = full of love); e più ampio si spazia = 'and more ample it extends/spreads' (si spazia = reflexive/impersonal 'it ranges/spreads', meaning the heaven's expanse is larger).
segments.purgatorio-26-g75: ditemi is the imperative 'tell me' (di' + mi), addressing them directly and asking for information.
segments.purgatorio-26-g76: acciò ch’ancor carte ne verghi = purpose clause 'so that I may still set it down on my pages' (acciò = so that/in order that; ch’ = that; ancor = still/yet; carte = pages/paper; ne verghi = 'write it in them' — verghi is a poetic/archaic infinitive 'to write' or 'to set down').
segments.purgatorio-26-g77: chi siete voi = direct question 'who are you' (chi = who; siete = you are; voi = you — formal/vocative plural).
segments.purgatorio-26-g78: e chi è quella turba che se ne va di retro a’ vostri terghi = 'and who is that throng which goes away behind your backs' (quella turba = that crowd/throng; che = which; se ne va = goes off/withdraws; di retro a’ vostri terghi = 'behind your backs' — terghi = backs/rear; Dante uses physical/metaphorical rear).
segments.purgatorio-26-g79: 'Non altrimenti' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'no differently / no less'; 'stupido' here means 'stupefied, stunned' (not merely 'stupid'); 'si turba' is a reflexive present indicative (3rd sing.) meaning 'becomes upset/disturbed'; word order places the predicate before the subject 'lo montanaro' for poetic emphasis: 'the mountaineer is just as disturbed.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g80: The coordinating 'e' ('and') links the action; 'rimirando' is a gerund ('while gazing/looking closely'); 'ammuta' (3rd sing. present) is an archaic/dialectal verb meaning 'becomes silent' or 'is struck dumb'. The construction = 'and, looking, falls silent.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g81: 'quando' introduces the subordinate temporal clause. 'rozzo e salvatico' are coordinate adjectives describing the man ('coarse and wild'); 's’inurba' is reflexive (s' = si) + present indicative of inurbare: here 'becomes urbane/civilized / is tamed' (i.e. made less savage).
segments.purgatorio-26-g82: The relative/result clause introduced by 'che' ('so that/so much that'): 'ciascun' = 'each', 'ombra' = 'shadow' (here: a shade/trace, i.e. expression), 'fece' is passato remoto of 'fare' ('made/caused'), and 'in sua paruta' (paruta = appeared) means 'appeared in his own countenance/appearance.' The phrase expresses a visible change in his look.
segments.purgatorio-26-g83: 'ma poi che' = 'but after that' (temporal/concessive). 'furon' is the archaic passato remoto 3rd pl. of 'essere' ('they were'); 'di stupore scarche' means 'rid/cleared of astonishment' (here 'scarche' functions like 'discharged/relieved'). The whole = 'but after they were relieved of astonishment.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g84: 'lo qual' is a relative pronoun referring back to 'stupore' (the astonishment); 'ne li alti cuor' = 'in their lofty hearts' ('ne' = in/of it/them, 'li' the archaic plural article, 'alti cuor' = high/lofty hearts); 'tosto' = 'soon/quickly'; 's’attuta' (reflexive) = 'is/was quelled, soothed, lessened'. So: 'which soon quieted itself in their lofty hearts.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g85: A vocative/exclamatory address: 'Beato te' = 'Blessed are you' (literally 'blessed you'). 'Beato' is an adjective used as a predicative/exclamation; 'te' is the direct vocative 'you.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g86: 'che' introduces a relative clause ('who/that'); 'de le nostre marche' = 'of/from our marches' (marche = borderlands, region). The phrase qualifies the person being addressed: 'blessed are you who come from our marches.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g87: 'ricominciò' is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of 'ricominciare' = 'resumed' or 'began again'; 'colei che pria m’inchiese' = 'she who earlier asked me' (colei = 'she who/the one', 'pria' = 'earlier', 'm’inchiese' = past of 'in chieder' with clitic 'mi' = 'asked me'). Word order places the relative clause after the verb for narration.
segments.purgatorio-26-g88: 'per' + infinitive 'morir' expresses purpose: 'in order to die (better)'; 'meglio' is the comparative/adverb 'better'. The phrase is an exhortation: 'to die better.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g89: 'esperïenza' = 'experience'; 'imbarche' is an archaic/thoughtful verb form (ending -e) that functions optatively/subjunctively or as an exhortation: roughly 'embark' or 'let [experience] embark' — here the speaker urges taking on experience. The exact morphosyntax is poetically compressed: an exclamatory jussive/subjunctive.
segments.purgatorio-26-g90: 'La gente' = 'the people'; 'che non vien con noi' is a relative clause ('who do not come with us'); 'vien' is an archaic present form ('viene'). The whole: 'the people who do not come with us.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g91: 'offese di ciò' = 'offended/angered at this'; 'per che' is an archaic conjunction meaning 'because'; 'già Cesar' = 'for Caesar already…' — the clause introduces the cause of their offence. 'offese' is predicative (agreeing with 'gente').
segments.purgatorio-26-g92: 'trïunfando' is a gerund (or present participle) 'triumphing' modifying Caesar: 'Caesar, triumphing…'. It gives the manner/context for the following infinitive clause.
segments.purgatorio-26-g93: Quoted infinitive phrase + impersonal verb: '“Regina” contra sé chiamar s’intese' = 'it was understood that he intended/carefully meant to call "Queen" against himself.' 'chiamar' is infinitive; 's'intese' (passive/impersonal past) = 'it was understood/it was meant.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g94: 'però' = 'therefore/for that reason'; 'si parton' = reflexive present 3rd pl. 'they depart/leave'; the quoted cry '“Soddoma”' is the shout they give; 'gridando' (gerund) = 'shouting'. The sequence describes their reaction.
segments.purgatorio-26-g95: 'rimproverando a sé' (gerund + prepositional phrase) = 'reproaching themselves'; 'com’ hai udito' = 'as you have heard' (direct address to Dante: 'as you heard'). 'hai udito' is 2nd singular perfect 'you have heard.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g96: Coordinating 'e' = 'and'; 'aiutan' (present 3rd pl.) = 'they help/assist' or 'they increase' (here 'aid' the burning); 'l’arsura' = 'the burning/torment/heat' (direct object); 'vergognando' = gerund 'being ashamed' or 'with shame'. The clause pictures them shamefully adding to the torment.
segments.purgatorio-26-g97: 'Nostro peccato fu ermafrodito' = predicate sentence with past tense 'fu' (3rd sing. past of 'essere'): 'Our sin was hermaphroditic.' 'ermafrodito' functions as predicate noun/adjective describing the nature of their sin.
segments.purgatorio-26-g98: 'ma perché' = 'but because'; 'non servammo' is an archaic passato remoto 1st pl. (we did not serve/observe/obey) used here with 'umana legge' = 'human law' (i.e. social/natural law). The sense: 'but because we did not keep human law.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g99: 'seguendo' is a gerund 'following'; 'come bestie' = 'like beasts'; 'l’appetito' = 'the appetite/desire' (direct object). Grammar: gerund phrase explains how they behaved — 'following appetite like beasts.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g100: 'in obbrobrio di noi' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to our shame/disgrace' (obbrobrio = disgrace/abomination); it comments on the moral evaluation of their deeds.
segments.purgatorio-26-g101: 'per noi si legge' uses the impersonal passive 'si legge' = 'is read/is written' and 'per noi' = 'concerning/us' or 'about us'; together: 'it is written of us' or 'of us it is read.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g102: 'quando partinci' — archaic phrasing: 'quando' = 'when'; 'partinci' is an irregular/archaic form (probably referring to leaving/departing). The clause gives the time: 'when you depart' / 'when you go away'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g103: 'il nome di colei' = 'the name of that one (she)'; 'che s’imbestiò' = relative clause 'who became beastly/enraged' (s' = reflexive); 'ne le ’mbestiate schegge' = 'in/among the beastified/feral shards/fragments' — a poetic, compressed image meaning she was turned into/become bestial amid the bestial fragments. The clause identifies the name written against them.
segments.purgatorio-26-g104: 'Or' = 'now'; 'sai' is 2nd-singular present 'you know' addressing Dante; 'nostri atti' = 'our deeds'; 'e di che fummo rei' = 'and of what we were guilty' — 'fummo' is an archaic past tense of 'essere' ('we were'), and 'rei' = 'guilty'.
segments.purgatorio-26-g105: 'se forse a nome vuo’ saper chi semo' = conditional: 'if perhaps you wish to know by name who we are.' 'vuo’' is the archaic 2nd-singular 'you want' (vuoi); 'saper' is the infinitive 'to know'; 'semo' is the archaic 1st-plural 'we are.'
segments.purgatorio-26-g106: 'tempo non è di dire' is a fixed idiomatic phrase: 'there is no time to say / it is not the time to tell' — 'tempo' = time, 'non è di' + infinitive indicates the absence of opportunity to perform the action.
segments.purgatorio-26-g107: 'e non saprei' — 'and I would not know (how)' or idiomatically 'and I couldn't' — 'saprei' is the conditional (1st sing.) of 'sapere'; here it expresses inability or lack of knowledge to tell the names even if asked.

sentences.purgatorio-26-s34: Then he hid himself in the fire that refines him.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s17: It will be to your advantage to think me foolish: I am Guido Guinizzelli, and already I am purging myself by rightly lamenting before my end.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s18: Just as, in Lycurgus’s sorrow, two sons were moved to see their mother again, so did I act; but I did not go so far when I hear the father himself named — my father and my other betters, who more than ever used sweet and graceful love-rhymes — and without hearing or speaking I, thoughtful, walked for a long time gazing at him, nor, because of the fire, did I come any closer.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s19: After I was satisfied with looking, I offered myself completely ready to his service, with the assertion that makes others believe.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s20: And he to me: 'You leave such a trace, by what I hear, in me, and so clear, that Lethe cannot take it away nor make it dull.'
sentences.purgatorio-26-s21: But if your words are now truly sworn, tell me what is the reason that shows, in your speech and in your looks, that you hold me dear.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s22: And I to him: 'Your sweet words, while the present custom endures, will still make their gatherings dear.'
sentences.purgatorio-26-s23: 'O brother,' he said, 'this one whom I single out to you with my finger,' and he pointed to a spirit in front, 'was a finer craftsman in motherly speech.'
sentences.purgatorio-26-s24: He outstripped everyone in love verses and prose of romances; and let fools say that the man from Limousin, they think, comes out ahead.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s1: While thus along the rim, one after another, we went on, and often my good master would say to me, "Look: it is fitting that I warn you"; the sun struck me on my right shoulder, which, already radiating, was turning the whole west into a pale, sky‑blue aspect; and with the hottest shadow I made the flame appear; and yet at that signal I saw many shades take notice as we went.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s2: This was the cause that set them talking about me; and they began to say, "That one does not seem a feigned body"; then toward me, as much as they could, some assumed a fierce bearing, always taking care not to step where they would not be burned.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s3: "O you who go — not out of slowness, but perhaps out of reverence, after the others — answer me, for I burn with thirst and with fire."
sentences.purgatorio-26-s4: Your answer is needed not only by me; for all these have a greater thirst for it than for the cold water of the Indus or of Ethiopia.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s25: They heed public opinion more than the truth, and thus fix their judgment before art or reason can be heard by them.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s26: Many of the ancients acted the same toward Guittone, passing praise of him from shout to shout, until the truth, supported by more people, prevailed over him.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s27: Now if you have so ample a privilege that you are allowed to go to the cloister in which Christ is abbot of the college, say for me one Our Father, as much as is necessary for us in this world, where it is no longer in our power to sin.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s28: Then, perhaps to make room for another who was near him, he vanished into the fire, like a fish going to the bottom through the water.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s5: Tell me how it is that you turn yourself toward the sun, just as if you had not yet entered death’s net.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s6: Thus one of them spoke to me; and I would already have revealed myself, if I had not been held back by another novelty that then appeared; for through the middle of the blazing path people came with their faces toward this one, which caused me to suspend my gaze.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s7: There I see, from every side, each shade make itself ready and kiss one another, one with another, without staying, pleased with a brief festival; thus, within their dark/ brown rank one ant mingles with another, perhaps to spy out their way and their fortune.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s8: No sooner has the friendly welcome departed than, before the first step there is taken, each one strains to cry out: the new folk cry 'Sodom and Gomorrah'; and the others cry 'Pasiphae enters the cow, so that the young bull may run to his lust.'
sentences.purgatorio-26-s30: He began freely to say: "Your courteous request so pleases me that I can neither, nor will I, hide myself from you."
sentences.purgatorio-26-s31: I am Arnaut, who weeps and goes singing; I see the past folly as I consider it, and I see the joy that I hope for beforehand.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s32: Now I beg you, by that valor that guides you to the top of the little stair, remember my sorrow in time!
sentences.purgatorio-26-s29: I stepped a little forward toward the one who had been shown, and I said that at the sound of his name my desire was preparing a gracious place.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s9: Then, like cranes that from the Rife mountains fly off in part and in part toward the sands — some driven away by frost, others scorched by the sun — one band goes away, the other comes; and they return, weeping, to the first chants and to the cry that best suits them; and they draw near to me, as before, the very same ones who had begged me, intent on listening from their faces.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s10: I, who had twice seen their favor, began: 'O confident souls of having — whenever it may be — a state of peace, my limbs over there were not left unripe nor mature, but they are here with me with their blood and with their joints.'
sentences.purgatorio-26-s11: From here upward I go so as not to be blind any more; there is a lady above who wins grace for me, through whom I carry the mortal through your world.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s12: But if your greater desire is soon satisfied, so that heaven may lodge you — it is full of love and spreads out more widely — tell me, so that I may still write it down on my pages, who you are, and who is that throng that goes off behind your backs.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s13: No less is the mountaineer disturbed, and, gazing, falls silent when a coarse, wild man is made civilized, so that every shadow showed itself in his countenance; but after they had been rid of astonishment — which soon quieted in their lofty hearts — “Blessed are you, who from our marches…,” resumed she who had asked me before, “embark on experience to die better!”
sentences.purgatorio-26-s14: The people who do not come with us, offended at that because Caesar, triumphing, was understood to intend to call 'Queen' against himself: therefore they depart crying 'Sodom', reproaching themselves as you have heard, and, ashamed, they further the burning.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s15: Our sin was hermaphroditic; but because we did not observe human law, following appetite like beasts, to our shame it is written (of us) — when you depart — the name of that one who became beastly among the beastly shards.
sentences.purgatorio-26-s16: Now you know our deeds and what we were guilty of: if perhaps you wish to know by name who we are, there is no time to tell, and I could not.
